title: Te Awa Tupua: The Living River Ecosystem | Te Awa Tupua: Te Pūnaha Hauropi Ora
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will explore the Mangakōtukutuku Stream through both mātauranga Māori and Western science lenses, connecting to Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau about the stream as a life-giving force and the traditional practices of monitoring its health through tuna (eel) populations and water clarity observations.
learningObjectives: Measure and analyze water quality parameters (pH, turbidity, temperature) of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream using scientific instruments
Compare traditional Ngāti Kahungunu indicators of stream health (tuna migration patterns, invertebrate presence) with scientific water quality data
Create a collaborative mural that visually represents both mātauranga Māori and scientific understandings of a healthy river ecosystem
Propose evidence-based conservation actions to protect the Mangakōtukutuku Stream based on cultural and scientific findings

activities.title: Whakatau me te Whakawhanaungatanga | Welcome and Connection to Place
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to the Mangakōtukutuku Stream. Local kaumātua shares pūrākau of the stream and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. Students create observational drawings of the stream from different vantage points, noting both natural features and cultural markers.

activities.title: He Rongoā Taiao | Environmental Monitoring
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students collect and analyze water samples from three points along the stream (upstream, mid-stream, near confluence). They measure pH, turbidity, temperature and dissolved oxygen, recording data in field journals. Simultaneously, they search for traditional indicators: presence of tuna, kōura, and certain riparian plants.

activities.title: Ngā Kōrero o te Awa | Stories of the River
activities.description: Students interview local knowledge holders (via arranged visits or recorded videos) about historical changes in the stream, traditional monitoring practices, and cultural uses. They compare these qualitative observations with their quantitative scientific data looking for patterns and connections.

activities.title: Te Hā o te Awa | Breath of the River Mural
activities.description: In small groups, students create a collaborative mural on large canvas or paper. One half depicts the stream's health through a mātauranga Māori lens (using symbols, patterns, and narratives), the other half through a scientific lens (using data visualizations, graphs, and diagrams). Groups present their murals explaining the connections between both knowledge systems.

resources: Local Kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu (e.g., Aunty Hine Smith) for pūrākau and traditional ecological knowledge
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) resources and equipment
Digital copies of 'Te Kawa o Te Awa' - Ngāti Kahungunu environmental management plans
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's environmental monitoring data portal for Mangakōtukutuku Stream

tasks: Completed field journal with water quality measurements and traditional observations
Group mural presentation explaining connections between cultural and scientific data
Written proposal for one stream conservation action supported by evidence from both knowledge systems

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Understanding about science - Students will learn about science as a way of investigating the natural world that exists alongside other knowledge systems
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic interactions with other Earth systems
Key Competencies: Participating and contributing - Students will contribute to the care of their local environment
